text: Posterior retroperitoneoscopic adrenalectomy, the right surgery for the right patient. The authors present a case of a sixty-four years old male, with past medical history of Hypertension, medicated with three different antihypertensive drugs. Rectal resection for rectal cancer, with permanent colostomy, in April two thousand and eighteen. And bladder cancer, high grade T-one, diagnosed after derp surgery, in June two thousand and nineteen. During the staging of the bladder cancer, abdominal pelvic CT scan, found a right adrenal gland tumor, with four and a half centimeters. The patient was totally asymptomatic, and had no findings in physical examination. The metabolic study, with evaluation of plasmatic and urinary metanephrines, confirmed the presence of a full functioning adrenal gland tumor.

The patient is positioned in the genopectoral position with bent hips and knees for a right posterior retroperitoneoscopic adrenalectomy. A transverse incision is made below the tip of the twelfth rib, followed by blunt and sharp dissection to access the retroperitoneal space. A small cavity is prepared digitally for the insertion of a 5mm trocar laterally beneath the eleventh rib, and a 10mm trocar medially below the twelfth rib. A blunt trocar with an inflatable balloon and adjustable sleeve is introduced into the initial incision site and secured.

The patient was discharged on the second day after surgery. No complications were recorded. One month after surgery, the patient had the first follow-up consultation. The metabolic study confirmed normalization of both plasmatic and urinary metanephrines. In conclusion, with this video, the authors demonstrate the feasibility and safety of a successful, posterior retroperitoneoscopic adrenalectomy, in a patient with past history of radical abdominal surgery.
